text:
**Organization:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)
**Date:** 2023
**Source:** https://cpcb.nic.in/National-Air-Quality-Index/
section_heading: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: The National Air Quality Index (NAQI) developed by CPCB categories measured pollutant concentrations into six categories, each with associated health impacts and advisory messaging.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: Minimal impact. Normal activities recommended.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: May cause minor breathing discomfort to sensitive people. Sensitive individuals should monitor for symptoms.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: May cause breathing discomfort to people with lung disease, heart disease, children, and older adults. Sensitive groups should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: May cause breathing discomfort to people on prolonged exposure and discomfort to people with heart or lung disease. Children, elderly, and people with respiratory or cardiac conditions should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion. Schools should consider moving intense outdoor activity indoors.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: May cause respiratory illness to people on prolonged exposure. Avoid prolonged outdoor exertion for everyone. Sensitive groups should remain indoors where possible.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: May cause respiratory effects even in healthy people and serious health impacts in people with lung or heart disease. Avoid all outdoor exertion. Emergency-level air quality.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text: CPCB recommends that state pollution control boards issue public health advisories when AQI reaches Poor or above. Advisories should target sensitive groups including children, elderly, and people with pre-existing respiratory or cardiac conditions.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text:
While AQI categories indicate likely pollution levels, they do not identify specific emission sources. Source apportionment requires additional monitoring and dispersion modelling studies beyond routine AQI measurement.

This document is a DEMO placeholder. Replace with the official CPCB NAQI document before enabling citations.
title: CPCB National Air Quality Index: Categories and Health Advisory

text:
**Organization:** World Health Organization
**Date:** September 22, 2021
**Source:** https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240034228
section_heading: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text: The WHO air quality guidelines (AQG) provide recommended limits for PM2.5 concentrations based on systematic review of health evidence.
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text: Annual mean PM2.5 should not exceed 5 µg/m³.
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text: 24-hour mean PM2.5 should not exceed 15 µg/m³ for no more than 3–4 days per year.
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text:
Short-term exposure to elevated PM2.5 is associated with:
- Increased daily mortality
- Exacerbation of respiratory and cardiovascular disease
- Increased hospital admissions for respiratory conditions
- Worsening of asthma symptoms

Long-term exposure is associated with:
- Reduced life expectancy
- Increased cardiovascular mortality
- Development of chronic respiratory conditions
- Adverse birth outcomes
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text: Children, older adults, and individuals with pre-existing cardiovascular or respiratory disease are at increased risk from PM2.5 exposure. These groups should take additional precautions during high-pollution episodes.
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text:
During episodes where PM2.5 exceeds guideline levels, WHO recommends:
- Reducing physical activity outdoors, especially prolonged or strenuous activities
- Sensitive individuals should remain indoors where possible
- Using clean indoor air sources if available
- Following national and local authority guidance

This document is a DEMO placeholder. Replace with the official WHO AQG document before enabling citations.
title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines: Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)

text:
**Organization:** Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Government of India
**Date:** 2024
**Source:** https://moef.gov.in/ncap/
section_heading: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP): City Action Plan Guidelines
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

text: The National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) is a national-level strategy aiming to reduce particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) concentration by 40% by 2025-26 compared to 2017-18 levels, with 2017 as the base year.
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

text:
Cities with industrial zones should implement:
- Regular compliance inspections of industrial emission control systems
- Monitoring of fugitive dust from industrial processes
- Enforcement of emission standards for industrial stacks
- Promotion of cleaner fuel switching
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

text:
Cities should address traffic-related pollution through:
- Strengthening vehicle emission testing and enforcement
- Promoting public transport and non-motorized transport
- Traffic management to reduce congestion and idling
- Control of road dust through mechanical sweeping and water sprinkling
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

text:
Action plans must include:
- Enforcement of dust control measures at construction sites
- Covering of construction materials and debris
- Water sprinkling at demolition sites
- Compliance with CPCB construction dust guidelines
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

text:
Cities should establish:
- Continuous ambient air quality monitoring stations
- Source apportionment studies to identify local emission sources
- Public grievance redressal mechanisms for air quality complaints
- Quarterly review of action plan implementation
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

text:
State pollution control boards are responsible for conducting inspections of industrial, construction, and other pollution sources. Prioritization should be based on:
- Emission potential and compliance history
- Proximity to sensitive receptors (schools, hospitals, residential areas)
- Contribution to city-level PM concentrations
- Public complaints and reported violations

This document is a DEMO placeholder. Replace with the official NCAP document before enabling citations.
title: National Clean Air Programme: City Action Plan Guidelines
organization: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
